results.possibleIdentity:
The name 'Otto Tambellini Jan?' likely refers to a combination or confusion of two prominent artists who were frequent collaborators in the late 1960s and early 1970s: Otto Piene and Aldo Tambellini. Both artists were pioneers in the 'electromedia' movement and were closely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). 

Otto Piene (1928–2014) was a German artist and co-founder of the ZERO movement. He was a pioneer in using light, smoke, and air as artistic media and later served as the director of the Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) at MIT, which fostered many E.A.T.-style collaborations between artists and engineers. 

Aldo Tambellini (1930–2020) was an Italian-American artist known for his 'Black' series of films, paintings, and performances. He co-founded the Black Gate Theatre in New York, a venue dedicated to electromedia and experimental performance.

In 1968, the two collaborated on 'Black Gate Cologne,' a live television art broadcast on WDR in Germany, which is considered one of the first television programs to be produced by experimental visual artists. They also collaborated on 'Black Air' (1968) and were featured in the influential WGBH program 'The Medium is the Medium' (1969) alongside Nam June Paik and Allan Kaprow. The suffix 'Jan' in the request may refer to Jan van der Marck, a curator who was instrumental in the E.A.T. network and organized early exhibitions such as 'The Machine: As Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' (1968), or it could stem from Jan Dibbets, a conceptual artist who also participated in the early television art scene (e.g., 'TV as a Fireplace,' 1969).

webSearchQueries: "Jan" artist associated with Otto Piene and Aldo Tambellini
"Jan van der Marck" E.A.T. Piene Tambellini

results.possibleIdentity: Dolores H. "Dilly" Schapiro (1930–2022) was a New York-based environmental designer, architect, and arts patron active during the late 1960s and 1970s. Born in New York City and raised in Brooklyn, she attended Smith College (BA 1951) and later earned a bachelor's degree in environmental design from the Parsons School of Design in 1973. She also studied at the Columbia University School of Architecture. During the 1970s, she worked for various New York architectural firms and served on the staff of the West Side Highway Project. Her professional background in environmental design and architecture aligns with the multidisciplinary collaborations fostered by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that connected artists with engineers and designers. She was a prominent arts enthusiast and longtime patron of major institutions, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

webSearchQueries: "Dolores Shapiro" Experiments in Art and Technology
